ground_truth.title: The average cost of therapy in America by state
ground_truth.author: Jess Barron
ground_truth.main_content: When considering the cost of therapy—you might think a session is most expensive in California or New York. Think again. Our deep dive into SimplePractice exclusive data reveals therapy pricing patterns that may defy conventional wisdom. According to SimplePractice internal data, the cost of therapy has risen almost 13% over the past five years. In 2019, therapists charged an average of $123 for therapy sessions (both private pay and insurance). In 2024, the average cost of therapy has risen to roughly $139—a $16 increase. The market rate, also known as the going rate for a therapy session, refers to the range of rates which vary by city and state. In 2023-2024, the average fee per therapy session varied by state and region—from as low as $122, to as high as $227, according to SimplePractice data. The cost disparity is particularly evident at the top of our list, where two states stand out with average cost of therapy sessions exceeding $200: North Dakota at $227 and Alaska at $212. The geographic distribution of therapy costs challenges simplistic cost-of-living explanations. While metropolitan hubs with higher costs of living contribute to their states ranking among those with the highest rates for therapy sessions, the presence of rural states like North and South Dakota at the very top of our rankings reveals a more complex reality: the critical variable appears to be provider scarcity, rather than regional affluence. Based on SimplePractice data, in 2023-2024, only six U.S. states had rates of therapy under $130 per session: Oklahoma, Tennessee, Vermont, South Carolina, Louisiana, and Missouri. The 10 states with the lowest costs of therapy include: Missouri at $122, Louisiana at $123, South Carolina at $123, Vermont at $126, Tennessee at $126, Oklahoma at $129, Texas at $131, Indiana at $132, Nevada at $134, and Florida at $134.80.
